central_thesis: Multiple agentic products can compete for the same paid-plan included usage allowance and shared purchased credits. When one product consumes the pool, less remains for the others.
technology_acknowledgment: GPT-5.6 Thinking's exceptional reasoning, research, writing, evidence-organization and development capabilities were used to create this public project.
thanks: The project sincerely thanks OpenAI's researchers, engineers, safety teams and product staff for their work and continued improvements.
method: Claims are classified as documented fact, reasonable inference, consumer position, or not established. Primary OpenAI sources are preferred.

claims.claim: Codex, ChatGPT Work, ChatGPT for Excel and Workspace Agents draw from the same agentic usage and credit pool when those features are available.
A plan's included usage is used first. After the limit is reached, supported usage can draw from purchased credits where available.
Credits purchased through Codex can be consumed by other supported features, including ChatGPT Work and ChatGPT for Excel.
If an eligible user enables Auto top-up, consumption across supported products can lower the shared balance and trigger a purchase under the user's configured settings.
OpenAI says content from individual services may be used to train and improve models unless the user opts out, and that real-world problems and data help improve accuracy, capabilities and safety.
When multiple products draw from one finite allowance, use by one product leaves less included capacity for the others and may lead users to ration work, wait, upgrade or buy credits.
Paying users do not need to demand wages or ownership to reasonably expect clear disclosure, adequate capacity and protection against silent dilution of a paid work allowance.
The available public evidence does not establish that the shared-pool design was adopted specifically to increase margins or prove OpenAI's internal financial intent.
It is not accurate to say every conversation trains every model. Individual users can opt out, Temporary Chats are excluded, and business products are opted out by default.

OpenAI states that most new and existing customers across Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise, Health, Edu and Gov have been migrated from legacy per-message Codex pricing to token-based credit pricing; a small subset of Enterprise customers remains on the legacy rate card.
OpenAI states that a typical Codex task using GPT-5.5 may consume approximately 5 to 45 credits, while actual use varies by task and configuration.

For ChatGPT Business, OpenAI states that ChatGPT Work, Workspace Agents, ChatGPT for Excel and ChatGPT for PowerPoint draw from the same agentic usage and credit pool. PowerPoint charging begins on 6 August 2026.
The public documentation does not establish the exact financial loss or gain for an individual subscriber because consumption varies by model, tokens, context, tools, task complexity and product mix.
